summary: O'Neil predicts Facebook's decline, arguing that the platform has become too bland and commercialized to attract younger users who want to escape parental oversight. She contends that Facebook's success in welcoming parents and making the platform inoffensive has paradoxically sealed its fate with the next generation. The post extends this analysis to email, suggesting it's being replaced by texting as the privileged form of communication, with even newer, more exclusive communication methods likely to emerge.
key_points: Facebook's parent-friendly design has made it unappealing to younger users who see it as another space for authority figures to monitor them
A new social platform will eventually capture the younger generation who want distance from family and teachers
Email is losing its status as a privileged communication channel because too many people have access to each other's addresses
Future communication may require additional authentication beyond contact information to signal message importance
money_quotes: Facebook is boring. Super boring, and super commercial and getting worse.
It's used anyway, by almost everyone, because everyone else they care about is already on it.
Inoffensive to the point of being utterly bland.

The kids today are just not going to be interested in adopting Facebook. Why should they, when their parents and teachers and coaches are on there already, and it's just another place where they can get into trouble with authority?

My theory is that some other sexier and edgier social media platform is going to come along and sweep up the next generation of users who want to get the hell away from their parents and grandparents.
Too many people know each others' email addresses, so knowing someone's phone number is really the only sign of authentic connection.
key_insight: Platforms that optimize for mass adoption and safety inevitably become too bland for the next generation, who will flee to wherever their parents aren't.
